entity_name: Top 19 Automotive Manufacturing Companies in Georgia
facts: Georgia has emerged as a significant hub for automotive manufacturing, housing a mix of traditional and innovative companies.
Firms in the state are involved in the production of vehicles, parts, and electric components.
Advancements in technology drive efficiency, while automation reshapes workforce dynamics.
Georgia's automotive sector is well-equipped to handle a shift towards sustainability.
The state continues to invest heavily in fostering workforce skills pertinent to the evolving automotive landscape.
Most companies highlighted were founded before the 2000s, indicating stable growth and commitment to innovation.
Many operations focus on electric vehicle components, reflecting a transition toward greener alternatives.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, Inc. (KMMG) is an automotive manufacturer located in West Point, Georgia.
KMMG represents Kia's first manufacturing site in North America.
The facility spans 2,200 acres and has an annual production capacity of 340,000 vehicles.
KMMG began mass production on November 16, 2009.
It has produced over 4.2 million vehicles since starting mass production.
The plant assembles the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, Sportage SUV, and K5 mid-size sedan.
KMMG recently started production of the all-electric EV9.
The company has created thousands of jobs and contributes to community development through outreach initiatives.
Kia Georgia, Inc. is an automotive manufacturer located in West Point, Georgia.
Kia Georgia serves as Kia Corporation's first manufacturing site in North America.
The facility began mass production in 2009 and has produced over 4.2 million vehicles.
It specializes in assembling the Telluride SUV, Sorento SUV, Sportage SUV, and K5 mid-size sedan.
Kia Georgia recently expanded production capabilities to include the all-electric EV9.
The company operates on a 2,200-acre site with an annual production capacity of 350,000 units.
It is committed to community engagement, contributing to local economic development and workforce training initiatives.
Mercedes-Benz USA is based in Atlanta, Georgia.
It is a prominent automobile manufacturer and luxury vehicle retailer founded in 1965.
The company specializes in electric models, SUVs, and sedans.
It has a workforce of over 11,000 employees.
The company is actively involved in the electric vehicle market.
Haering Precision USA LP is a family-owned company based in Lavonia, Georgia.
Established in 1961, it specializes in precision turned parts for automotive applications.
Product offerings include components for traditional combustion engines, hybrid and electric vehicles, and hydrogen fuel cells.
The company operates internationally with significant presence in Poland, Germany, and Tunisia.
Jefferson Southern Corporation is based in Rockmart, Georgia.
Founded in 2001, it specializes in high-quality stamped and welded components for vehicles.
It operates as a Tier One supplier providing parts ready to be installed in automobiles.
Products primarily form part of the vehicle's interior shell.
The company is part of the North American J-group, which includes operations in Georgia, Ohio, and Canada.
It is affiliated with G-TEKT.
Ajin Georgia is a private automotive parts manufacturer based in Register, Georgia.
Founded in 2023, it is part of the South Korea-based Ajin Corporation.
The company is constructing a factory in Bulloch County.
The new factory is expected to create over 600 jobs.
The investment for the new facility is approximately $278 million.
Operations will support the Hyundai Motor Group's Metaplant.
The company donated $40,000 to local organizations before commencing production.
Sewon America Inc. is based in Rincon, Georgia.
Established in 1985, it specializes in automotive stamping and interior parts.
The company maintains a robust research and development framework to enhance manufacturing processes.
Mack Trucks INC is based in Fayetteville, Georgia.
It has manufactured heavy-duty trucks since 1900.
Truck models include the Mack Anthem and Mack Granite.
The company provides fleet management and maintenance services.
It develops electric vehicle options such as the Mack LR Electric.
It supports customers with 24/7 roadside assistance through the Mack OneCall program.
Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC - Georgia Plant was established in 2007 and operates out of West Point, Georgia.
The plant specializes in assembling and supplying chassis modules, cockpit modules, and front-end modules.
It plays a vital role in supporting the KIA Georgia factory.
The company employs cutting-edge vehicle modularization technology and a just-in-sequence manufacturing environment.
Toyota Industries Group TACG-TICA was established in 2004 and is based in Pendergrass, Georgia.
The company specializes in producing vehicle air conditioning compressors and DC-AC inverters.
It has a workforce of approximately 1,300 associates.
It produces around 4.6 million air conditioning compressors annually.
In 2020, it expanded operations to include the assembly of DC-AC inverters.
Ecoplastic America Corporation was founded in 1966 and is based in Register, Georgia.
It specializes in eco-friendly plastic components for vehicles.
It serves as a tier-1 supplier for Hyundai Motor Group.
Products include bumpers, consoles, trims, and plastic molds.
The company is actively building a manufacturing facility in Bulloch County, Georgia.
Trenton Pressing was founded in 1995 and located in Trenton, Georgia.
It specializes in engineered stamping and welding assembly solutions for the automotive industry.
The company operates from over 192,000 square feet across three facilities.
It is a direct Tier One supplier to major manufacturers including BMW and Club Car.
The company is IATF-certified.
SK battery America is based in Commerce, Georgia.
Founded in 2019, it specializes in electric vehicle batteries.
It has a workforce of over 3,000 employees.
The company focuses on high-nickel technology for superior energy density and extended driving range.
It is dedicated to sustainability through the development of high-efficiency lithium-ion batteries.
KTX America Corporation, also known as NIFCO KTW America, is based in Toccoa, Georgia.
Founded in 2016, it specializes in high-quality plastic components for vehicle interiors and exteriors.
It is a tier 1 supplier to major manufacturers such as BMW and Daimler.
The company employs advanced manufacturing techniques including multi-component injection molding and high-gloss painting.
Its production facility handles both small and large-scale series production.
Porsche Cars North America is based in Atlanta, Georgia.
Founded in 1948, it manufactures high-performance vehicles and luxury lifestyle products.
Product lineup includes the Macan, Taycan, and 911 models.
The company offers lifestyle products including eBikes and smartwatches.
It is making strides in the electric vehicle market.
Lyle Industries Inc was founded in 1981 and based in Dalton, Georgia.
It manufactures textile products for the automotive industry, including automotive carpet, molded flooring, and interior trim.
The company produces approximately 14 million square meters of carpet annually.
Yazaki Morocco is a subsidiary of Yazaki Europe specializing in electrical distribution systems and components.
Founded in 2000, it focuses on customized cabling systems, sub-harnesses, and electronic components for e-mobility applications.
Fouts Bros Inc. is a family-owned commercial vehicle manufacturer based in Milledgeville, Georgia.
Its history dates back to 1952.
It focuses on production and upfitting of work trucks, vans, and automotive equipment for public safety and military sectors.
Headquarters spans 700,000 square feet with a second facility in La Grange, Texas.
Rocky Ridge Trucks was founded in 1986 and based in Franklin Springs, Georgia.
It focuses on design and manufacture of custom and lifted 4x4 trucks.
It offers modifications for Chevrolet, Ford, GMC, Nissan, and RAM brands.
Modifications help maintain the factory warranty of original vehicles.

canonical_name: The Big Green Machine Search Database
facts: The Big Green Machine is a database tracking investments in clean energy manufacturing supply chains across North America (United States, Mexico, and Canada).
Users can search the database by site name, company, sector, operating status, city, state/province, country, or congressional district.
Duckyang Industrial Co. operates an EV sector facility in Braselton, Georgia (Congressional District GA9) with a capital investment of $10 million and a target of 285 jobs.
Duckyang Industrial Co.'s Braselton, Georgia facility is currently listed as operating.
Solarcycle has a planned solar glass facility in Cedartown, Georgia (Congressional District GA14) with a capital investment of $344 million and a target of 617 jobs.

entity_name: Georgia EV Battery & Supply Chain Ecosystem
canonical_name: Georgia EV Battery & Supply Chain Ecosystem
facts: Automakers in the Southern Automotive Corridor are optimistic that 2023 will be a rebound year after the worst year of vehicle sales in more than a decade.
Industry experts believe U.S. vehicle sales were near 14 million in 2022, an 8 to 9 percent drop from 2021.
Parts and supply chain issues affected many automakers in 2022.
Gov. Andy Beshear announced in January that economic development growth in Kentucky concluded its best two-year period for announced private-sector investment and job creation in state history.
In 2022, 248 private-sector new-location and expansion projects committed to invest nearly $10.5 billion and create 16,000 full-time jobs in Kentucky.
Those figures position 2022 as Kentucky’s second highest year for new investment behind 2021’s record year.

Kentucky’s average incentivized hourly wage for projects statewide in 2022 was $26.78 before benefits, an 11.5 percent increase over the 2021 mark of $24 and the second highest over an eight-year period.
Louisiana Gov. John Bel Edwards announced a new $1.8 billion container facility on the Lower Mississippi River in the winter quarter.
The Louisiana port expansion is a partnership among the Port of New Orleans, New Jersey-based Ports America, and Geneva, Switzerland-based Mediterranean Shipping Company.
The new Louisiana International Terminal (LIT) in St. Bernard Parish will be able to serve vessels of all sizes, dramatically increasing Louisiana’s import and export capacity.
The LIT project is expected to stimulate the creation of more than 17,000 new jobs statewide by 2050.
TVA announced in the fall quarter it is taking its first step in turning coal ash landfills into solar energy farms.
TVA’s board approved a $216 million investment for a pilot project to build a 100-megawatt, 309-acre solar farm on top of a coal ash landfill in Paducah, Ky.
The new TVA solar energy facility will be capable of powering about 600,000 homes.
Clean Hydrogen Works announced it is exploring a plan to build a large-scale hydrogen-ammonia production and export facility in Ascension Parish, La.
Doing business as Ascension Clean Energy, the project is in partnership with Denbury Carbon Solutions and Hafnia.
The company estimates the proposed $7.5 billion project would create 350 new direct jobs with an estimated average annual salary of $73,412 by 2030.
Louisiana Economic Development estimates 1,122 new indirect jobs would result from the hydrogen project, for 1,472 total potential new jobs in the Capital Region.
The federal government has selected the first two areas for offshore wind development in the Gulf of Mexico.
The U.S. Bureau of Ocean Energy Management set boundaries for the two zones: a 174,000-acre area south of Lake Charles and a 508,000-acre area near Galveston, Texas.
Virginia Gov. Glenn Youngkin is allocating $350 million for industrial site development in the Commonwealth called Virginia Business Ready Sites.
The new allocation would bring the state’s total recent investments for site development to $500 million.
Decatur, Ala., located on the Tennessee River, has always been a haven for top-line manufacturers, such as United Launch Alliance and 3M.
According to SmartAsset, Decatur is the second best place to work in manufacturing in the U.S., behind only Ames, Iowa.
Mazda and Toyota announced a joint-venture plant in Limestone County, Ala. back in 2018.

The Mazda/Toyota plant currently houses 3,700 employees, but the two Japanese automakers are having a tough time reaching the goal of 4,000 workers as a result of thin labor pools and retention issues.
Mazda makes its CX-50 crossover model at the Alabama plant and Toyota makes its Corolla Cross model there.

In the fall quarter, Gov. Kay Ivey joined executives of Hyundai Mobis to announce the company’s plans to invest $205 million to open an EV battery module plant in Montgomery that will eventually employ at least 400 people.

Once it reaches full production, the 450,000-square-foot facility will be able to supply over 200,000 EV batteries annually to the Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama (HMMA) factory in Montgomery and the Kia Georgia plant in West Point, Ga.

Gov. Brian P. Kemp announced in December that Hyundai Motor Group and SK On have selected a site in Bartow County, Ga., for a new electric vehicle battery manufacturing facility that will supply Hyundai Motor Group’s plants in the U.S.

One of the largest economic development projects in state history, stakeholders estimate it will create more than 3,500 new jobs through approximately $4 billion to $5 billion of investment in Bartow County.

Hyundai Motor Group and SK On recently signed a memorandum of understanding regarding the partnership for a new EV battery facility in the U.S., with the details of the partnership still in development.

Hyundai Mobis plans to invest $926 million in a new facility in Bryan County, Ga. to help supply electric vehicle production at Hyundai’s new multi-billion-dollar, multi-thousand-job complex in Southeast Georgia.
The Hyundai Mobis Bryan County deal will create 1,500 jobs.
FREYR Battery will invest $2.57 billion into Georgia’s sustainable technology ecosystem and create 723 new jobs over the next seven years at a manufacturing facility in Coweta County.
Joon Georgia Inc., an automotive parts manufacturer, will create 630 new jobs and invest $317 million in Bulloch County.
Plans for Joon Georgia’s new manufacturing facility mark the first confirmed supplier for Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America in Bryan County.

At the Glendale Megasite on Interstate 65 in Kentucky, Ford and its South Korean partner, SK, are building a pair of manufacturing plants that will produce batteries for Ford and Lincoln electric vehicles.
Construction has been going on for quite a while now on the multi-billion-dollar project, yet, the official groundbreaking took place in December.
The 2.3-square-mile campus will eventually employ 5,000 workers.

VinFast, which is building a $2 billion electric vehicle and battery plant in North Carolina, has filed a registration statement with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, the first formal step toward a public offering this year.
The automaker became Vietnam’s first automaker in 2019.
Canoo announced in the fall it will locate an assembly facility in Oklahoma City.
The Canoo project is expected to generate 500 jobs and 20,000 units by the end of 2023.
Redwood Materials is locating its next Battery Materials Campus, in the heart of the “Battery Belt,” just outside of Charleston, S.C., at Camp Hall in Berkeley County.
Redwood will recycle, refine and manufacture anode and cathode components on more than 600 acres, creating more than 1,500 jobs and investing $3.5 billion in the local community.
The company combines recycling, refining and remanufacturing to produce and return battery materials to U.S. battery cell manufacturers used in electric vehicles.
Ultium Cells, a joint venture of LG Energy Solution and General Motors, will expand its Spring Hill, Tenn., battery cell manufacturing operations.
The project represents a $275 million investment by Ultium Cells, which is in addition to the $2.3 billion investment announced in April 2021, and is expected to create 400 additional jobs.
Once the facility is fully operational, Ultium Cells is expected to employ a workforce of 1,700 in Maury County.
Ultium Cells will increase battery cell production by more than 40 percent, from 35 gigawatt-hours to 50 GWh.

The Tennessee facility will supply battery cells to General Motors’ Spring Hill assembly plant for production of the Cadillac LYRIQ, the first electric vehicle produced at the plant, and other GM Ultium Platform-based electric vehicles.
Production at the 2.8-million-square-foot facility will begin in late 2023.
LG Chem announced in December a $3.2 billion, cathode manufacturing facility in Clarksville, Tenn.
The LG Chem facility is part of the massive electric vehicle supply chain that is remaking much of the American South’s economy.
In the winter quarter, Austin-based Tesla delivered its first electric semi-truck to its first customer, PepsiCo.
The delivery is the first since Tesla CEO Elon Musk said his company would start making the trucks.
Switzerland-based Beyond Gravity is adding a new production facility in Decatur to provide additional payload fairings for the United Launch Alliance’s next-generation Alabama-built Vulcan rockets.
Beyond Gravity said it is working with ULA to build the new production facility, which will add 200 workers at the site in Morgan County.
The new hiring will essentially double the company’s headcount in Alabama.

Beyond Gravity — formerly known as RUAG Space — said the expansion project follows its selection by ULA to supply 38 payload fairings for Vulcan rockets launching satellites as part of Amazon’s Project Kuiper.
United Launch Alliance (ULA) announced in December it is spending $300 million to expand its rocket manufacturing facility in Decatur, Ala., the world’s largest rocket plant.
The expansion will allow ULA to nearly double its launch rate.

First Solar Inc. plans to invest $1.1 billion to establish a photovoltaic (PV) solar module manufacturing facility in Lawrence County, Ala., creating over 700 jobs in North Alabama, according to an announcement by Governor Kay Ivey.
The new factory is part of the company’s previously announced investment in scaling its American manufacturing footprint to over 10 gigawatts by 2025.
Highbar announced in the fall it will build a rebar mill on a 600-acre site that includes space for an expanded Mississippi River port facility, a railroad spur and a planned solar farm.
Groundbreaking is scheduled for the first half of 2023 once permits are obtained.
The Highbar deal will create 200 jobs in Mississippi County, Ark.

Dassault Falcon Jet announced it will build a new maintenance facility at Melbourne Orlando International Airport as part of the company’s global expansion of its maintenance, repair and overhaul network.

Intended to serve customers across North and South America and beyond, the 175,000-square-foot complex will accommodate all current Falcon models (including the new, ultra-long-range Falcon 10X, the largest purpose-built business jet), and will be capable of performing major maintenance and modifications on up to 18 Falcon models simultaneously.
Additionally, the site will be home to a 54,000-square-foot paint shop.
Construction in Melbourne is expected to begin in the second quarter of 2023 and opening is set for late 2024.

Archer Aviation, an aerospace company advancing sustainable urban air mobility, will create 1,000 jobs and invest $118 million over 10 years in a new state-of-the-art electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL) manufacturing facility located alongside the Covington Municipal Airport in Newton County, Ga.

Absolics, a subsidiary of SKC Co. Ltd., broke ground on a planned $600 million investment in Covington, Ga., for a new manufacturing facility that will supply advanced materials to the U.S. semiconductor industry.

The investment will create more than 400 high-skilled jobs in Georgia and help strengthen the U.S. semiconductor supply chain by manufacturing a new material that supports next-generation computing systems.
Charlotte-based Nucor rolled out its first steel plate in late December at its new plant in Brandenburg, Ky.
The first product from the factory has been shipped to customers.
The plant houses 400 workers.
Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear announced that Sazerac, one of the largest spirits distillers in the world, will invest $600 million to build about 20 new barrel warehouses.
The barrel storage facilities will be located in Laurel County.
AppHarvest, a sustainable food company, recently opened the world’s largest high-tech indoor farm in Somerset, Ky.
The new facility will grow cucumbers and strawberries.
As a result of higher natural gas prices and demand, drillers are returning in significant numbers to rural areas of Northwest Louisiana for the first time in a decade.
The war in Ukraine has sent Germany and other countries scrambling for gas due to Russian supply cuts, and demand is also rising in Asia.
That has pushed natural gas prices to some of the highest levels since 2008.
Gov. Tate Reeves signed legislation recently to finalize Steel Dynamics’ $2.5 billion expansion in Mississippi.
The project will create 1,000 new jobs.

Leaders of Catawba County, N.C., the cities of Conover and Hickory, the town of Maiden, and the Catawba County Economic Development Corporation (EDC) announced in the fall quarter that Microsoft plans to invest a minimum of $1 billion in the phased development of four data centers in Catawba County over the next 10 years.
The data centers will be located in Conover, Hickory and Maiden, and will create at least 50 new jobs.
Mexico-based Premium Aerospace Center plans to hire as many as 600 employees by 2026 at the Oklahoma Air & Space Port complex in Burns Flat, Okla., about 100 miles from Oklahoma City.
The company will build new hangers for aircraft painting and interior remodeling.
Finisar, a company that makes lasers for facial recognition technology in Apple’s iPhone, is considering Sherman for a new $3 billion semiconductor plant.
The new plant, an expansion to its current 76-acre site, would produce wafers for semiconductor chips and create 700 jobs.
Chevron Phillips Chemical Company and QatarEnergy announced they are proceeding with the construction of an $8.5 billion integrated polymers facility in Orange, Texas.
The facility is expected to create more than 500 full-time jobs and about 4,500 construction jobs and generate an estimated $50 billion for the community in residual economic impacts.
STIHL plans to invest $49 million to expand its chain saw guide bar manufacturing facility in Virginia Beach.
The expansion will add 26,000 square feet of space to an existing building, totaling 86,000 square feet upon completion.
The project is also expected to create 15 new jobs with an average annual wage of $60,000 to $100,000.
M S International, a California-based flooring, countertop, wall tile and hardscaping products supplier, will invest $61.6 million to establish its East Coast distribution facility in Suffolk.
The deal will create 80 jobs.
TRISO fuel produced at BWX Technologies’ Lynchburg facility in Virginia will power the Project Pele microreactor – the first microreactor to be built and operated in the USA.

Eastman Chemical Company is a global specialty materials company headquartered in Kingsport, Tennessee, with 41 manufacturing facilities and equity interests in three manufacturing joint ventures across 12 countries.
The company supplies Saflex™ PVB and HUD acoustic interlayers to the automotive sector, which are essential for passenger safety and advance improvements in solar, heat, and ultraviolet management.

Solar-absorbing Saflex™ PVB interlayer solutions, including the Saflex™ S series, Saflex™ Solar Connect, and XIR™ Solar Control Technology, are ideal for electric vehicle ("EV") glass in cabin-forward designs and larger sunroofs.
These EV glass interlayers reduce loads on air-conditioning systems and help maximize EV driving range.
The Saflex™ Q series advanced acoustic interlayers enable weight reduction of vehicles.
Tetrashield™ performance polyester resins provide improved performance and sustainability features for automotive coatings end-users.

Eastman's CORE analytics-based software platform provides automotive groups and professional installers access to shop management and automotive film patterns to improve customer experience and accelerate category development.

Eastman began construction of a methanolysis manufacturing plant in Kingsport, Tennessee, expected to be mechanically complete by the end of 2022, as part of its molecular recycling and circular economy initiatives.
Eastman's molecular recycling processes are expected to reduce greenhouse gas ("GHG") emissions by 20 percent to 50 percent when compared to processes using fossil fuels in various Renew products.

The company has committed to reducing its absolute scope 1 (direct GHG emissions occurring from sources that are owned by Eastman) and scope 2 (indirect GHG associated with the purchase of electricity, steam, heat, or cooling and are a result of Eastman's energy use) emissions by one-third by 2030 to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050.

canonical_name: Foreign Entity of Concern Interpretive Guidance
facts: In May 2024, the Department of Energy (DOE) issued its final interpretive guidance on the statutory definition of “foreign entity of concern” (FEOC) in the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).
The guidance is designed to reduce reliance on FEOCs in battery supply chains and bolster the growth of domestic and friend-shored battery materials and manufacturing.
The guidance is largely finalized as proposed in December 2023 with refinements and clarification to aid automakers and other stakeholders in identifying FEOCs in their battery supply chains.
The DOE final guidance applies to DOE’s Battery Materials Processing and Manufacturing grant program authorized by section 40207 of IIJA.
The program imposes limits when an entity’s battery supply chain includes foreign entities of concern.

A foreign entity is defined as a “foreign entity of concern” if it is “owned by, controlled by, or subject to the jurisdiction or direction of a government of a foreign country that is a covered nation.”
Covered nations are defined as Peoples Republic of China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea.

The final interpretive guidance clarifies the definition of “foreign entity of concern” by providing interpretations of four key terms: “government of a foreign country,” “foreign entity,” “subject to the jurisdiction,” and “owned by, controlled by, or subject to the direction.”

DOE’s interpretive guidance provides clarity on how to identify entities that are FEOCs as a result of being owned by, controlled by, or subject to the jurisdiction or direction of the government of a covered nation.
An entity incorporated in, headquartered in, or performing relevant activities in a covered nation is subject to the jurisdiction of a covered nation and is therefore a FEOC.

A company with at least 25 percent voting rights, board seats, or equity interests held by the government of a covered nation (including a senior official of such a government or dominant political party) is a FEOC, regardless of where those activities occur.

DOE’s guidance establishes a set of rights that an entity may reserve within contractual agreements to eliminate the potential for effective control if that entity has entered into a license agreement or contract with a FEOC.
There is no consolidated list of all entities considered FEOCs under section 40207(a)(5) of IIJA.

The statute identifies certain entities as FEOCs if they appear on lists maintained by other agencies, such as entities designated as foreign terrorist organizations by the Secretary of State and individuals/entities on the SDN List maintained by OFAC.
Individuals alleged by the Attorney General to have been involved in illegal activities like espionage and arms exports, for which a conviction was obtained, are also identified as FEOCs.
The term “covered nation” is defined in 10 U.S.C. 4872(f)(2) to mean North Korea, the People’s Republic of China (PRC), Russia, and Iran.

DOE interprets “foreign entity” to include a government of a foreign country, a natural person who is not a lawful permanent resident or citizen of the US, a partnership/corporation organized under foreign laws or with principal business in a foreign country, or a US entity owned/controlled by such an entity.
The 25 percent ownership threshold was set because it is a widely used standard to identify control by a parent company in the private sector.
Regulatory contexts using the 25 percent threshold include the Department of Commerce’s CHIPS rules, the Department of State’s ITAR, and the Treasury Department’s BSA private banking regulation.

A U.S. subsidiary of a foreign company is generally only considered a FEOC if it is headquartered/incorporated in a covered nation or at least 25 percent of its voting interest, board seats, or equity interests are held by a government of a covered nation.

The policy objective behind the guidance is to understand and mitigate the influence of a covered nation's government in the battery and critical mineral supply chain by assessing each entity’s relationship to that government.
DOE intentionally included specific reference to the National People’s Congress as an example of an entity whose current and former members qualify as “senior foreign political figures.”
To determine if an official is “senior,” they must be or have been in a position of substantial authority over policy, operations, or the use of government-owned resources.

Current and former members of the National People’s Congress satisfy the substantial authority standard and qualify as senior foreign political figures due to its transparent list of members serving five-year terms.
The final guidance omitted specific reference to the National Party Congress but anticipates participants will likely meet the substantial authority standard.
DOE's FEOC guidance is very similar to the CHIPS rule, with intentional distinctions driven by differences between battery and semiconductor markets and IIJA vs. CHIPS and Science Act.
DOE and the Department of Commerce coordinated throughout development and largely adopted the same interpretation of jurisdiction, ownership, and control.

DOE’s guidance considers an entity a FEOC under three scenarios: via jurisdiction of a covered nation's government, via 25% ownership/control by a covered nation's government, and via certain licenses or contracts with a FEOC.
The third provision addressing effective control through license/contract agreements is not found in the CHIPS rule and addresses means of control specific to the battery supply chain.
DOE’s guidance presents a strong signal that the U.S. does not see suppliers based in the PRC as part of a reliable or secure energy supply chain.
PRC-headquartered companies, PRC state-owned enterprises, and companies controlled by the PRC government are treated as FEOCs.

This includes any company producing an applicable mineral or battery component in China, as well as companies subject to 25 percent PRC government control through voting rights, board seats, or equity interests.

Individuals holding or having held senior positions within the PRC government or dominant ruling party (Chinese Communist Party), and their family members, are considered extensions of government control.
An entity not based in China or directly/indirectly controlled by the PRC government may still be a FEOC based on effective control through licensing agreements with another FEOC.

DOE will continue to closely monitor the market for PRC government influence and may incorporate non-ownership measures like direct/indirect investments to inform potential designation of entities as FEOCs under IIJA Section 40207(a)(5)(E).
source_title: Foreign Entity of Concern Interpretive Guidance | Department of Energy

entity_name: Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit (48C) Program
canonical_name: Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit (48C) Program
facts: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (P.L. 119-21) or Working Families Tax Cut, enacted on July 4, 2025, revised dates and provisions in the Internal Revenue Code that affect some or all of the tax credits described below.

The Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit (48C) was originally established by the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009 and subsequently renewed and expanded under the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (IRA).

The 48C credit is a tax credit for investments in advanced energy projects, as defined in 26 USC § 48C(c)(1) and is intended to build clean energy supply chains, drive investments, and lower costs in energy communities.
The IRA provided $10 billion in funding for the expanded 48C(e) Qualifying Advanced Energy Project Credit Allocation Program (48C(e) program).
To receive the full value of a 48C credit, projects must meet prevailing wage and registered apprenticeship standards.

The Department of the Treasury and the Internal Revenue Service, in partnership with DOE, conducted two rounds of tax credit allocations for projects that expand clean energy manufacturing and recycling and critical materials refining, processing and recycling, and for projects that reduce greenhouse gas emissions at industrial facilities.
The 48C Program is managed by the IRS with assistance from Department of Energy’s (DOE) Office of Manufacturing & Energy Supply Chains (MESC).

On March 29, 2024, the IRS allocated approximately $4 billion of 48C credits for over 100 projects in approximately 30 states, with approximately $1.5 billion allocated to projects in designated energy communities.

On January 10, 2025, the IRS allocated approximately $6 billion of the 48C credits for over 140 projects in approximately 30 states, with approximately $2.5 billion allocated to projects in designated energy communities.

Clean Energy Manufacturing and Recycling Projects involve re-equipping, expanding, or establishing an industrial or manufacturing facility that manufactures or recycles one or more of the specified advanced energy properties outlined in the Round 2 guidance.

Production of energy-intensive materials that have a substantially lower carbon intensity when compared to an appropriate industry-specific benchmark are now eligible under the Clean Energy Manufacturing and Recycling Projects category.

Industrial Decarbonization Projects (formerly named Greenhouse Gas Emissions Reduction Projects) qualify if they involve retrofitting an industrial or manufacturing facility, particularly in energy-intensive sectors such as cement, iron and steel, aluminum, and chemicals, with equipment designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at least 20 percent.

Critical Materials Projects involve re-equipping, expanding, or establishing an industrial facility for the processing, refining, or recycling of critical materials (as defined in § 7002(a) of the Energy Act of 2020).
To claim a 48C credit, allocated projects must meet certification and placed in service requirements within two years of receiving an Allocation Letter.
Awardees must submit information through the 48C portal notifying DOE that the certification requirements have been met.
The IRS certifies the 48C Facility by sending a Certification Letter after DOE notifies the taxpayer and the IRS that it has received the taxpayer’s notification.
Within an additional two years of receiving the Certification Letter, the taxpayer must notify DOE that the facility has been placed in service via the 48C Portal.
Failure to meet any of these milestones will result in allocated 48C credits being forfeited.
The 48C(e) Program will publish the names of all organizations with certified projects and the amount of that allocation after projects are certified.
The program provides an investment tax credit of up to 30% of qualified investments for certified projects that meet prevailing wage and apprenticeship requirements.
Approximately $1.6 billion of this allocation will be set aside for projects in designated energy communities.

Section 48C(e) Energy Communities Census Tracts are census tracts in which no pre-IRA 48C projects were located and a coal mine has closed after December 31, 1999 or a coal-fired electric generating unit has been retired after December 31, 2009 or which is directly adjoining to any of these census tracts.
Effective February 29, 2024, the Qualifying Advanced Energy Tax Credit Portal is migrating from eXCHANGE to a new system, the 48C Portal, requiring ID.me login instead of Login.gov.
Round 2 full application submissions were due by 11:59 p.m. EST on October 18, 2024.
Round 2 concept paper submissions were due by 5 pm EST on June 21, 2024.
The 48C Portal opened for concept paper submission at 9 am EST on May 22, 2024.
Narrative templates for applications could not exceed 30 pages, and workforce and community engagement plans could not exceed 5 pages.
Applicants were required to submit a business entity certification with their application.
An organization that submitted a Round 2 concept paper was permitted to submit a full application regardless of whether they received an encourage or discourage notification.
DOE hosted an informational webinar on November 28, 2023, at 12pm ET for the application process.
DOE and Treasury previously hosted a virtual informational webinar for potential applicants on June 27, 2023, at 2 p.m. ET.
A Round 2 full applications informational webinar was held on September 16, 2024, at 1:00 p.m. Eastern Time.

The Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains is recruiting Round 2 reviewers for the merit review process, seeking expertise in commercialization, supply chains, industrial decarbonization, and workforce and community engagement.
There are three anticipated groups of reviewers for each application: Special Purpose Reviewers, Super Reviewers, and General Reviewers.
Special Purpose Reviewers are expected to spend approximately 3-5 hours per application and receive $300-$500 per application plus $500 for administrative activities.

Four Super Reviewers review each application, spending approximately 6-8 hours per application plus up to 1-2 days for meetings, receiving $800-$1,000 per application plus $500 for administrative activities.

General Reviewers review the complete application and all criteria, spending approximately 8-12 hours per application plus up to 1-2 days for meetings, receiving $800-$1,200 per application plus $500 for administrative activities.

All reviewers are required to sign the Nondisclosure Agreement (NDA) and Conflict of Interest and Foreign Government-Sponsored Talent Recruitment Program Involvement forms, in addition to IRS Data Privacy forms.
All reviewers are required to submit for background checks including fingerprinting and background investigation before they can be considered for merit review of applications.
KBC Energy Solutions, LLC is supporting the Department of Energy Office of Manufacturing and Energy Supply Chains in reviewer onboarding.

Directory lists 107 automotive stamping companies across the United States.
Last updated in January 2026.

Buyers in this category typically solve critical problems such as achieving tight geometric tolerances on safety-critical components, optimizing material utilization for cost-sensitive parts, and managing tool wear on high-volume progressive dies.
Buyers also seek partners proficient in consistent material flow and thinning control for deep drawing operations with challenging materials.

Supplier types range from small job shops ideal for prototyping or lower-volume components using single-station dies, to mid-sized facilities specializing in multi-stage progressive dies with in-house secondary operations, to large Tier 1 manufacturers offering high-volume production, automated transfer presses, welding, assembly, and expertise in AHSS and aluminum alloys.

Buyers should prepare comprehensive CAD models (STEP, native SOLIDWORKS/CATIA files preferred), precise material specifications (gauge, grade, temper), clear annual volume forecasts, and target piece price or tooling budget.
Suppliers expect a detailed understanding of the part's function, critical characteristics, assembly requirements, and realistic timelines for tool build and PPAP submission.

A significant trend in automotive stamping is the continued drive towards lightweighting, leading to increased demand for expertise in stamping advanced high-strength steels (AHSS) and aluminum, often requiring hot stamping or specialized springback compensation techniques.

The rise of electric vehicles fuels demand for complex battery enclosure components, pushing innovation in press force monitoring, automation integration, and precision forming of intricate geometries.

facts: The HL-GA Battery Company joint venture is located in Bryan County, Georgia.
The facility represents a $4.3 billion investment.
The facility has a 30 GWh annual production capacity.
The project was announced in May 2023 as a cornerstone of US EV supply chain localisation.
A September 4, 2025 ICE operation detained 475 workers at the site.
More than 300 of the detained workers were South Korean nationals.
The raid created a critical labour shortfall at a facility reported to be 98% complete.
By April 2026, Hyundai CEO José Muñoz stated the plant was ready to open.
Muñoz credited a Korean-style recovery push that kept the H1 2026 production timeline intact.
On September 4, 2025, more than 500 federal, state, and local agents descended on the construction site in Bryan County, Georgia.
HL-GA Battery Company is a 50/50 joint venture between Hyundai Motor Group and LG Energy Solution.
The JV represents one of the largest single battery manufacturing investments in US history.
Homeland Security Investigations Special Agent in Charge Steven Schrank described it as the largest single-site enforcement operation in the agency's two-decade history.
On April 14, 2026, Hyundai CEO José Muñoz stood at the Semafor World Economy conference in Washington, D.C. and told the audience the plant was ready to open.
Muñoz stated: "We applied the Korean concept, which we call pali-pali; it means hurry, hurry. And we were able to catch up. So we are launching on time."
Benchmark Minerals Intelligence noted in late April 2026 that the facility is "central to localising electric vehicle supply chains."
The HL-GA Battery Company plant is located in Ellabell, Georgia, approximately 25 miles west of Savannah.
The project was announced via a Seoul signing ceremony on May 26, 2023.
The JV committed to hire at least 8,500 workers by the end of 2031.
State and local governments pledged $2.1 billion in tax breaks and related incentives.
A subsequent August 2023 announcement raised the investment total further.
The combined value of Hyundai's Bryan County commitments, encompassing both the battery JV and the adjacent Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America EV assembly plant, reached $7.59 billion.
Hyundai's total Georgia footprint, including a separate SK On battery joint venture in Commerce, stands at approximately $12.6 billion.
The scale positions Georgia as the single most important geography for Hyundai's North American manufacturing strategy.
The Inflation Reduction Act's domestic content requirements created powerful incentives to produce battery cells and packs inside the United States.
Hyundai Mobis would assemble battery packs from the Ellabell cells and supply them to HMGMA for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EV models.
Construction began in the second half of 2023.
The original production target was late 2025.
The target was revised before the September raid to early 2026 due to what LG Energy Solution described at the time as "market conditions."
The actual opening is confirmed for the first half of 2026.
The ICE-led operation on September 4, 2025, involved agencies including Homeland Security Investigations, the Georgia State Patrol, the FBI, the DEA, and the ATF.
Approximately 50 detained workers worked directly for LG Energy Solution.
Roughly 250 detained workers were employed by HL-GA Battery Company LLC.
ICE's Schrank stated that all 475 detainees were illegally present in the United States, citing a combination of illegal entry, overstayed visas, and visa waiver holders prohibited from working.

Lawyers for the detainees argued that many held valid B-1 business visas and were lawfully present to install and commission specialised equipment under standard practices Korean firms had used routinely.
With H-1B and L-1 visas chronically undersupplied, Korean engineering firms had for years rotated technical staff through 90-day ESTA entries or B-1 visas to meet construction schedules.
KED Global noted this practice was widespread across Korean FDI projects in the United States.
The original search warrant named four individuals.
Workers were held at an ICE processing facility in Folkston, Georgia, for seven days before being released under voluntary departure terms that avoided formal deportation records.
South Korea chartered a Korean Air Boeing 747-8I to repatriate 316 South Korean nationals.
A brief standoff over whether workers would be transported in handcuffs was resolved after Seoul's objection prompted what reports described as a White House-level reversal.
The immediate industrial consequence was a complete work stoppage.
LG Energy Solution suspended all US business travel and halted construction "until further notice."
Hyundai imposed a parallel travel ban.
CEO Muñoz publicly confirmed on September 15 that the raid would delay the plant's opening by "at least two to three months."
Hyundai spokesperson Michael Stewart subsequently confirmed the revised target as H1 2026.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun flew to Washington within days of the raid to meet Secretary of State Marco Rubio and negotiate the workers' release.
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung, who had met with President Trump at the White House less than two weeks before the raid, described the incident as "bewildering."
Lee Jae Myung issued a direct warning: "As things stand now, our businesses will hesitate to make direct investments in the United States."
At least 22 other South Korean projects in the United States, spanning automotive, shipbuilding, steel, and electrical equipment sectors, were effectively frozen in the raid's immediate aftermath.
Korean firms recalled staff and suspended US business travel across the board.
The Washington Post reported in November 2025 that several South Korean companies had pulled or extended pauses on US investment projects.

The disruption was particularly pointed given that South Korea had pledged $350 billion in US investment as part of a July 2025 trade deal that reduced Trump's tariffs on Korean goods from 25% to 15%.
Hyundai's chairman had committed to a $26 billion US investment through 2028.

Jenny Town, director of the Korea program at the Stimson Center, stated: "The longer this situation goes on, the less likely companies will want to bring their employees into the United States. It's completely self-defeating for what the Trump administration has been lobbying other countries to do as far as investing in the U.S."

The Carnegie Endowment for International Peace noted that enforcement actions targeting flagship investment projects created "strategic whiplash" for Korean officials repositioning away from China toward deeper US economic integration.
White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles personally called Muñoz to apologise, saying the White House "didn't know what happened and they were going to do everything they could."
Governor Brian Kemp, who was unaware of the operation, expressed frustration and pledged to work toward resolution.

President Trump, in a Fox News interview, defended bringing South Korean battery engineers to the United States, arguing that battery production is "very complicated" and "very dangerous," and that engineers who have been "making batteries all their lives" serve a legitimate industrial function.

Immigration attorney Charles Kuck told Forbes that the operation was "entirely driven by Stephen Miller's arrest quota," a reference to the White House-imposed target of 3,000 immigrant arrests per day.
The United States and South Korea agreed to establish a working group to develop a new visa category for corporate investment workers.
The long-stalled Partner with Korea Act, which would create an E-4 visa classification for specialised South Korean workers, received renewed legislative attention.
Experts estimated that at least 20,000 such visas annually would be needed to support the scale of Korean industrial FDI currently in the US pipeline.
By November 2025, approximately 50 of the detained Korean engineers had re-entered the United States.
More than 100 of the 317 detained South Korean nationals had their B-1 business visas reinstated without requiring full reapplication.
Lawyers arguing the workers' case cited the visa reinstatement as an implicit acknowledgment of their lawful presence.
Construction resumed after the visa reinstatements.
The facility was reported complete in December 2025.

Muñoz stated at the Semafor conference: "My top priority is the U.S.A.," he said. "If you can make it here in the United States, you can make it anywhere. When we do well here, you do well everywhere."
The plant's more than 500 current employees are predominantly local workers.
Technical experts from South Korea assisted with factory setup.
Hyundai sourced battery cells from alternative suppliers, including its Commerce, Georgia SK On joint venture, during the delay period.
Some areas of the battery production process had already begun operating by late April 2026.
The facility spans approximately 2.5 million square feet.
President Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act on July 4, 2025, eliminating the federal EV tax credit.
The federal EV tax credit had provided up to $7,500 for new EVs and $4,000 for used EVs.
The credit expired on September 30, 2025.
The immediate market response was a consumer rush in Q3 2025 followed by a sharp contraction.
US battery electric vehicle sales have fallen 22.6% year-to-date in 2026.
PHEV sales are down 52.8%.
Hybrid vehicles are up 7.8% year-to-date, continuing a trend that NADA chief economist Patrick Manzi noted saw hybrids gain 22% across all of 2025.
The broader North American EV market contracted 1% in 2025.
Global EV sales grew 21% to 18.5 million units in the first eleven months of the year, according to Rho Motion data.

Princeton University's Zero Lab analysis projects EV sales could be approximately 40% lower in 2030 without the now-eliminated credits compared to the trajectory they would have followed under the IRA's original 2032 horizon.
The World Resources Institute has documented at least $19.9 billion in planned US manufacturing investments canceled since the policy shifts began.
The American Security Project has estimated that policy reversals could shutter up to half of US EV factories over the next five years.
Ford scrapped the electric F-150 Lightning in favour of an extended-range hybrid in December 2025.
GM scaled back EV production in January 2026.
Honda cancelled three North American EV models in March 2026.
Hyundai announced plans in September 2025 to launch extended-range EVs capable of 600-plus miles of range by 2027, combining electric motors with gasoline backup.

Kang DaeKwun, chief investment officer at Life Asset Management, stated: "The case demonstrates how tough it has become for Korean companies to make money from the investments in the US. Return on investment was already getting low due to inflation, and now companies face hiring challenges as well."
LGES maintains a broad North American manufacturing footprint, including joint ventures with General Motors under the Ultium banner, with Stellantis, and with Honda.
The Georgia plant is one node in a multi-facility North American strategy rather than a single point of failure.
source_title: Pali-Pali: How the Hyundai-LGES Georgia Gigafactory Recovered from a 475-Person ICE Raid to Edge Toward H1 2026 Production - Silmaril Media

facts: Kia Corporation is a South Korean multinational automobile manufacturer headquartered in Seoul, South Korea.
It is South Korea's second-largest automobile manufacturer, after its parent company, Hyundai Motor Company, with sales of over 2.8 million vehicles in 2019.
Kia is owned by Hyundai, which holds a 33.88% stake valued at just over US$6 billion.
In October 2006, Kia Motors America broke ground for Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia in West Point, Georgia, representing a US$1 billion investment for the company.
Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia opened in February 2010.
In November 2009, Kia started production at the first U.S. Kia Motors plant, Kia Motors Manufacturing Georgia, in West Point.
The facility initially assembled only the Kia Sorento crossover vehicle.
The facility expanded its production lines to include the Kia Optima mid-size sedan, now sold as the K5, in 2011.
The facility added the Kia Telluride crossover SUV to its production lines in January 2019.
As of September 2019, the Georgia location had successfully built over 3 million units of these three models altogether.
The facility celebrated reaching its 10th year of production in November 2019.
Currently, the facility has a production capacity of 340,000 vehicles per year.
Production at the Georgia facility is largely dedicated to the Telluride and Sorento crossover SUVs.
The facility distributes assembled vehicles to hundreds of dealerships in the U.S. and Canada.
The facility fulfills shipments across North America and even overseas.
Kia Motors America (KMA), now known as Kia America, Inc., was incorporated in California on October 21, 1992, and became the American sales, marketing, and distribution arm of Kia Corporation.
Kia America is based in Irvine, California.
Kia Canada has increasingly focused on selling electric vehicles and plug-in hybrids.
Starting in 2005, Kia identified design as its "core future growth engine," leading to the hiring of Peter Schreyer in 2006 as chief design officer.
Kia started using an angular "KIA" wordmark logo and removed "Motors" from its corporate name to become Kia Corporation from January 2021 onwards.
Kia Motors has specialized in the production of military vehicles with variants and other transportation equipment since 1976.
Georgia's plastic manufacturing sector produces a wide range of plastic products for various industries, from automotive to consumer goods.
The industry increasingly prioritizes sustainability and recycling, responding to global demand for eco-friendly solutions and reducing waste.

Southern Polymer distributes polypropylene, polyethylene, and polystyrene for the automotive industry from Atlanta, Georgia, as part of the Audia Group.

KTX America utilizes advanced technologies including multi-component injection molding and high-gloss painting.
Elitech manufactures plastic components and molds for batteries from its Atlanta, Georgia location, employing over 150 people.

Thomson Plastics operates machines capable of exerting up to 3700 tons of clamping force.
OECHSLER Motion, Inc. provides plastic injection molding and additive manufacturing for the automotive sector from Acworth, Georgia.

facts: Continental AG is a German multinational manufacturing company headquartered in Hanover, Lower Saxony.

Following the spin-off of its automotive technology division as Aumovio in 2025, Continental now focuses on two core businesses: Tires and ContiTech (rubber and thermoplastic products for industrial customers).
The divisions Chassis and Safety, Powertrain, Interior and ADAS belonged to the Automotive segment, which was spun off as Vitesco (2021) and Aumovio (2025).
ContiTech is earmarked for sale in 2026.
Continental sells tires for automobiles, motorcycles, and bicycles worldwide under the Continental brand.

Continental also produces and sells other brands with more select distribution, such as Viking, General Tire (U.S./Canada), Gislaved Tires (Canada, Spain, Nordic Markets), Semperit Tyres, Barum to serve EU and Russia, Uniroyal (Europe), Sportiva, Mabor and Matador.

Continental's customers include all major automobile, truck and bus producers, such as Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz Group, BharatBenz, Ford, Volvo, Iveco, Schmitz, Koegel, Freightliner Trucks, BMW, General Motors, Toyota, Honda, Renault, Stellantis (ex-PSA) and Porsche.

The headquarters for North and South American tire divisions is located in the Charlotte metropolitan area at Lancaster County, South Carolina, United States (with a Fort Mill, South Carolina mailing address).
In 2011, Continental Tire the Americas announced that it would build a plant in Sumter, South Carolina.
The Sumter, South Carolina plant will cost about US$500million and employ 1,600 workers by 2020.
In February 2016, Continental Tire the Americas announced that it would build a Commercial Tire plant in Clinton, Mississippi.
The Clinton, Mississippi plant will have an investment totaling approximately US$1.4billion and employ 2,500 people when the plant reaches full capacity in the next decade.
In October 2016, Continental Tire the Americas purchased Hoosier Racing Tire.
Continental has a research and development arm in Silicon Valley where the company focuses on developing technologies supporting autonomous driving vehicles.

In April 2016, Continental AG together with Honda's U.S. subsidiary, were honored with the 2016 Automotive News PACE Innovation Partnership Award for the Bidirectional Long Range Communications (BLRC) System.

The BLRC Radio Frequency Device helps the car user to operate a remote control key fob from more than half a kilometer away, to start the engine and climate control function, while receiving feedback from the vehicle.

The Radio Frequency System, powered by a single standard coin cell, and an innovative vehicle-mounted RF transceiver, was developed together by Honda and Continental, and was debuted on the Acura MDX in 2013 and was quickly followed by the Acura TLX and Acura RLX in 2014.

In 2015, Continental AG was honored with two PACE Awards for its Bare Die High-Density-Interconnect (BD-HDI) Printed Circuit Board Substrate Technology for Transmission Electronics and its Multi-application Unified Sensor Element (MUSE).
In 2018, Continental won a PACE Award for its Digital Micromirror Head-Up Display technology.
Along with Audi, Continental also received an Innovation Partnership Award for their Safety Domain Control Unit (SDCU).
In 2020, Continental won an inaugural PACEpilot award for its Virtual A-Pillar technology that helps to eliminate forward blind spots.
On 1 January 2026 Christian Kötz was appointed CEO of Continental AG, succeeding Nikolai Setzer (who served until 31 December 2025).
In August 2025 Roland Welzbacher was appointed CFO of Continental AG.
In February 2026 Continental completed the sale of its OESL business unit.
The sale of ContiTech is planned for 2026.
Continental Tire entered the North American tire industry with its 1987 purchase of General Tire from GenCorp, Inc., forming Continental General Tire Corp.
In 2006, Continental announced that it would cease tire production in Charlotte and close its tire production plant in Mayfield, Kentucky.
Continental AG has acquired the American rubber company Veyance Technologies, Inc. based in Fairlawn, Ohio.
Veyance will be integrated into the company's ContiTech division, and will serve as the regional home office for ContiTech in North America.
The Brazilian antitrust authority Council for Economic Defence (CADE) made it official on 29 January 2015 that the total transition was US$1.6billion.

Continental will divest Veyance's NAFTA air springs business in Mexico and its Brazilian steel-cord belting business in response to antitrust concerns, employing about 600 people work in those operations.
ServisFirst Bancshares Inc. is a single bank BHC founded in Birmingham, Alabama.
The bank opened a branch in Atlanta, Georgia between 2012 and 2015 as part of its strategic growth across the Southeast.
As of March 31, 2026, ServisFirst operates 35 banking locations across eight states.

JTEKT Corporation is a Japanese multinational corporation specializing in the manufacture and sale of automotive components, bearings, and machine tools.
The company was formed on January 1, 2006, through the merger of Koyo Seiko Co., Ltd. and Toyoda Machine Works, Ltd.
JTEKT is headquartered in Kariya, Aichi Prefecture, Japan.
As of March 2025, consolidated operations employ 45,018 people globally.
The company generated net sales of 1,884,397 million yen for the fiscal year ended March 2025.
Core product lines include electric power steering (EPS) systems, constant velocity joints, precision bearings, high-performance machine tools, and electronic control devices.
JTEKT emphasizes innovation in sustainable mobility solutions to support advancements in electric vehicles and automated driving technologies.
In January 2010, JTEKT acquired the needle bearing business from The Timken Company in the United States.
In July 2011, JTEKT merged and acquired GKN JTEKT Ltd., integrating advanced driveline component technologies.
In October 2012, JTEKT merged Toyoda Machine Works Torsen Co., Ltd., enhancing expertise in torque-vectoring differentials and driveline systems.
In March 2014, JTEKT Automotiva Brasil Ltda. began manufacturing electric power steering systems in Brazil.
In September 2015, JTEKT Automotive Mexico, S.A. de C.V. commenced production of electric power steering in Mexico.
In June 2024, JTEKT opened the Carbon Neutrality (CN) Laboratory at its Kariya Plant in Japan for researching energy-saving technologies and low-carbon production methods.
In October 2025, JTEKT announced the integration of its steer-by-wire system and Libuddy® backup power supply into the Lexus RZ electric vehicle, marking the first such adoption in a Lexus model.
In October 2025, JTEKT formulated its "2030 Environmental Action Plan," aiming for carbon neutrality across Scopes 1, 2, and 3 by reducing CO2 emissions 60% from FY2013 levels by FY2030.
In August 2025, JTEKT completed the sale of its European needle roller bearing business to AEQUITA SE & Co. KGaA to streamline operations and enhance profitability.
In December 2023, JTEKT announced a $48.4 million investment in its Greenville, South Carolina, facility to add capacity for automotive components.
Operations at the Greenville, South Carolina, facility are planned to come online in the third quarter of 2025, creating 87 jobs.
JTEKT North America Corporation serves as the regional headquarters for JTEKT's operations in the Americas and is located in Greenville, South Carolina.
As of March 2025, North American operations employ 6,758 people across 23 facilities in the United States, Canada, and Mexico.

The company announced a $48.4 million expansion of its Greenville, South Carolina, manufacturing facility in 2023, adding seven new machining lines and two assembly lines focused on components for electric vehicles.
Operations at the expanded Greenville facility began in the third quarter of 2025, creating 87 new jobs.
JTEKT North America supplies components to major automakers including Ford, General Motors, and Toyota.
JTEKT's driveline components and steering products are adapted for electric vehicles, prioritizing noise reduction and lightweight materials to align with electrification trends.
The automotive division accounts for approximately 71% of the company's total revenue in fiscal year 2024 (ended March 31, 2025), with net sales of ¥1,333.1 billion.
JTEKT's electric power steering systems hold the world's top market share since their introduction in 1988.
The company produces steer-by-wire technologies like J-EPICS™ and Pairdriver™ that simulate mechanical steering feedback while incorporating redundant safety systems for autonomous driving.
JTEKT's machine tools division holds the global No. 1 market share of 55% in cylindrical grinders.
Research and development efforts prioritize sustainable machining processes, targeting carbon neutrality by 2035 and a 60% reduction in CO2 emissions by 2030 compared to 2013 levels.
JTEKT operates over 50 subsidiaries in Asia, including 22 in China, supporting high-volume production for the region's automotive sector while integrating EV and hybrid technologies.
In October 2024, JTEKT India announced its eighth facility in Gujarat to produce automotive parts, with completion targeted for 2027.
JTEKT maintains a European headquarters in Almere, Netherlands, overseeing sales and technical centers across the region.
As of March 2025, European operations employ 5,528 people across 21 companies.

In 2025, JTEKT completed the transfer of its European needle roller bearing business to AEQUITA SE & Co. KGaA, allowing consolidation on core automotive lines like electric power steering and driveline components.
JTEKT Automotive Morocco S.A.S., established in 2017, produces electric power steering with production starting in 2020 at an annual capacity of 230,000 units.
JTEKT operates a global network of 115 subsidiaries across multiple countries.
Novelis is the leading producer of flat-rolled aluminum products and the world's largest recycler of aluminum.
The company provides innovative solutions to the beverage can, automotive, and high-end specialty markets.
As of March 31, 2018, Novelis had manufacturing operations in ten countries on four continents through 24 operating facilities, including recycling operations in eleven of these plants.
Fiscal year 2018 shipment volumes reached 3,333 kt with net sales of $11,462 million for the year ended March 31, 2018.
All common shares are owned indirectly by Hindalco Industries Limited, which acquired Novelis in May 2007.
The company's principal executive offices are located at 3560 Lenox Road, Suite 2000, Atlanta, GA 30326.
Aluminum rolled products serve as raw material for vehicle structures (body-in-white) and automotive body panel applications including hoods, deck lids, fenders, and lift gates.
Growth in passenger and commercial vehicle applications is driven by lighter weight, better fuel economy, and improved emissions performance.
Novelis anticipates increased growth in the transportation end-use market due to government regulations requiring improved emissions and fuel economy.
Heat exchangers like radiators and air conditioners are key transportation applications for aluminum rolled products.
Original equipment manufacturers utilize specially treated aluminum sheet for interior and exterior automotive applications.
Novelis operates automotive finishing lines in North America, Europe, and Asia that are running near full production levels.
In February 2018, Novelis announced a greenfield expansion in Guthrie, Kentucky to add approximately 200 kt of additional automotive finishing capacity in North America.
Construction at the Guthrie, Kentucky facility is underway with an expected commissioning date in 2020.
The company defines recycled content as total scrap metal used in production less melt loss, increasing from 33% to 57% between fiscal 2011 and fiscal 2018.
Novelis collaborates with automotive customers to redesign alloys for higher recycled inputs and purchases aluminum scrap from closed-loop recycling systems.
Aluminum competes with steel and composites in transportation, with substitution trends accelerated by fuel efficiency regulations and carbon emission reduction goals.
The company's strategy focuses on defending its leadership in beverage can and automotive markets through quality, service, and innovative supply solutions.
Novelis aims to maximize shareholder value via free cash flow generation and increased return on capital employed.
The firm maintains a competitive cost structure by managing metal input costs and improving operational efficiencies across its global network.
Novelis is the only company of its size offering high-recycled content aluminum sheet for beverage and specialty product customers.

Primary competitors in the aluminum rolled products market include Alcoa, Arconic, Aleris, Constellium, Gränges, Maaden, UACJ, Amag Austria Metall, Companhia Brasileira de Alumínio, Hulamin, Norsk Hydro, and others across multiple regions.
Novelis reports financials in U.S. dollars using daily exchange rates published by Citibank.
The company faces competition from substitute materials such as glass, PET plastic, steel, wood, cement, magnesium, copper, and carbon fiber depending on the application.

Facet Srl is headquartered in Collegno, Piedmont, Italy, and was founded in 1946 as a family-owned business.
Facet Srl has a Family Owned ownership type and a headcount of 51-200.
Facet Srl specializes in automotive spare parts, sensors, ignition components, and thermal management systems with a catalog of over 4,500 products.
Facet Srl's sensors play a crucial role in engine management for European, Japanese, and Korean vehicle brands.
Facet Srl serves customers in over 100 countries with a significant portion of revenue from exports.
Hirschmann Automotive is headquartered in Rankweil, Vorarlberg, Austria, and was founded in 1959.
Hirschmann Automotive has a Corporate ownership type and a headcount of 5001-10000.
Hirschmann Automotive's employee distribution is Morocco 37%, Austria 30%, Romania 16%, and Other 16%.
Hirschmann Automotive secured funding in January 2018 and employs over 6,000 people globally.
Hirschmann Automotive designs connectors, cable assemblies, and sensors for conventional and electrified vehicles.
Hirschmann Automotive develops solutions supporting e-mobility and autonomous driving trends.
LDL Technology is headquartered in Ramonville-Saint-Agne, Occitanie, France, and was founded in 2004.
LDL Technology has a Private Equity ownership type and a headcount of 51-200.

LDL Technology specializes in TPMS and telematics solutions for trucks, buses, motorcycles, and sports cars.
LDL Technology holds over 25 active patents and partners with Harley Davidson, BMW, and Ducati.
LDL Technology became a member of the Amphenol global company in late 2023.
Fae is headquartered in L'Hospitalet De Llobregat, Catalonia, Spain, and was founded in 1952.
Fae has a private ownership type and a headcount of 201-500.
Fae employs around 500 people and offers over 5,000 product references including engine management sensors, thermal management components, and ignition systems.
Fae exports approximately 75% of its products to both OEM and independent aftermarket markets.
Nifco established a subsidiary named Nifco KTW America Corporation in Georgia, USA, in April 2015.
The company is a major manufacturer and supplier of plastic fasteners for the Japanese auto industry.

Nifco manufactures xEV powertrain components including breather units, LID modules, cooling pipes, filters/strainers, baffle plates/oil catch tanks, quick connectors, hydrogen combined piping for FCVs, inlet/outlets for FCVs, filters for FCVs, pressure relief valves, and battery spacers.

Nifco produces ADAS components such as camera covers, camera brackets, front millimeter-wave radar brackets, corner radar brackets, corner radar covers, corner radar protectors, sonar brackets, and clearance sonar retainers.

Nifco manufactures engine, fuel, and transmission parts including turbo ducts, engine oil separators, thrust washers/receivers, breather boxes, baffle plates/oil paths, engine oil strainers, oil control valve filters, quick connectors, cooling pipes, actuator inlets, filters, fill limit vent valves, inlet check valves, capless fillers, combo valves, rollover valves, baffle plates, nipples, lock nuts, push lifters, and outer handles.

Nifco supplies interior automotive components such as overhead consoles, cup holders, ECU contract items, seat back tables, seat related items, center console lids, center consoles, glove compartments, under-seat space items, luggage compartment products, assist grips, glove compartment dampers, inner handles, tonneau covers, luggage handles, luggage related items, air vents, sun-visor holders, clip alternatives to Hog rings, sunglass holders, tether clips, seat hooks, trim storage, and fasteners.

Nifco manufactures exterior automotive parts including corner seals, aerodynamics components, resin brackets, bumper retainers, quiet products, dust proofs, energy absorbers, plug holes, push lifters, lid modules, clips, clamps, fender seals, screw grommets, glass holders, cushion rubbers, shock absorbers, hole plugs, ECU brackets, outer handles, back door handles, deflectors, and fasteners.

Nifco produces automotive fasteners such as push turn rivets, push rivets, anchor clips, box anchor clips, brush clips, trim clips, screw grommets, hole plugs, bands, bands with clips, weather strip clips, linkage bushings, protectors, hood bumper rubbers, garnish clips, mat fasteners, connectors, bolt caps, scrivets, canoe clips, turn fasteners, push pull rivets, double clips, Niflocks, pipe clamps, cable clips, purse locks, cable straps, rod holders, latches & strikes, and pair clamps.
Nifco manufactures dampers including gear dampers, axis dampers, air dampers, linear dampers, latches, middle-torque gear dampers, and barrel dampers.
Nifco produces electrical components for automotive applications including ECU boxes and power supply ports.
source_title: Nifco Inc. - MarkLines Automotive Industry Portal

facts: Global H1-2025 power-battery installs reached ~504.4 GWh (+37.3% YoY), with LFP holding ~61% share and NMC concentrating in high-performance EVs.
China accounted for ~58.5% of global battery installs (~295.2 GWh, +48.8% YoY), while overseas markets reached ≈209.2 GWh (+23.8% YoY).
Continued localization in the US/EU is expected under IRA/CBAM with tighter origin rules.
Global lithium battery demand is projected to rise from ~471 GWh (2021) to ~3,939 GWh (2028E), implying a ~31% CAGR from 2023–2028.
EV & traction power demand remains the largest segment at ~66–71% of total market share.
Utility/Home-ESS demand is growing fastest, projected to reach 30% share (~1,182 GWh by 2028E).
CATL produced 96.7 GWh out of a global 296.8 GWh in the referenced period, up 167.5% yearly.
CATL introduced its Tener energy storage system on April 9, 2024, a standard 20-foot container-based LFP system claiming zero capacity/power loss for the first five years.
CATL unveiled a second-generation sodium-ion battery with 200Wh/kg energy density in November 2024, expected to launch in 2025.
LG Energy Solution is investing $5.5 billion in battery plants in the U.S.
LG Energy Solution's Ultium Cells JV operates in the U.S., debuting NA-made LFP for ESS.
LG Energy Solution expects tax breaks from the U.S. government to help achieve a 45 to 50 GWh production capacity.
LG Energy Solution introduced its next-generation pouch-type high-voltage mid-nickel CTP battery in September 2024, offering up to 600 km range for large trucks.
Panasonic secured the third spot globally outside China with 44.6 GWh supplied (14% market share) and plans to build four additional EV battery plants.
Panasonic's Kansas EV cell plant has a Phase 1 target of ~30–40 GWh/yr.
Samsung SDI announced 2023 sales of 22.71 trillion Korean won and is building a second U.S. battery plant in collaboration with Stellantis.
Samsung SDI expects the power battery market to grow ~18% in 2024 to approximately $184.8 billion.
Samsung SDI introduced the Samsung Battery Box (SBB) 1.5 on June 19, 2024, a 20-foot containerized LFP solution storing up to 5.26 MWh.
Samsung SDI unveiled an LFP+ battery in September 2024 optimized for commercial EVs, enabling an 80% charge in 20 minutes.
BYD announced plans to build a $1.2 billion EV battery plant in central China.
Tesla operates Megafactories in the U.S. and Shanghai for utility storage, with global deployments.
Tesla announced a Megapack 3 (~5 MWh class) in 2025.
MANLY Battery exports to the US/EU from facilities in Shenzhen/Dongguan/Huizhou, China.
SK On operates the BlueOval SK JV in the U.S. and announced an LFP ESS program for North America targeting mass production from 2H 2026.
CALB Group has CALB USA & EU supply operations and launched a 392 Ah ESS cell and a 6.25 MWh liquid-cooled 20-ft container system.
Gotion High-Tech targets 40 GWh cells + 10 GWh packs at its Manteno, IL site.
Northvolt's EU assets were acquired by Lyten in August 2025, with a Poland BESS (~6 GWh) restart plan.
A123 Systems has a US/CN/EU footprint and produces 12V/48V low-voltage automotive packs and utility/C&I platforms.
SVOLT canceled planned Saarland/Lauchhammer factories in 2024 but continues exporting ESS/EV products to overseas markets.
source_title: Top 19 Lithium Ion Battery Manufacturer in 2026 - MANLY Battery
